desc:
Two feet tall, lush and green grass cover this wide plain. Very few flowers,
mostly tiny pale ones, grow here. To the west the landscape elevates some as the
grass climbs numerous hillocks. South of here the grass grows taller, it is also
darker in colour. To the east a large area of tall hills stand, looking as if
they are cloaked in a layer of dark green pines.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
Brownish green grass grow tall on this open plain. In some spots the grass
disappears and only charred, cracked earth can be seen. The open area ranges
from here both to the north and to the south, the grass looking as if it is a
bit shorter to the north. Hills can be seen to the north-west and north-east,
and east of here a trail can be seen winding through the wooded hills. To the
west a very dark spruce forest stands.
name: Worn, Grassy Plains

desc:
A narrow, worn earthen trail leads into the rolling hills to the south and
further into the large fields of tall brown-green grass to the east. Small
clumps of young sallow trees grow in the western end of this plain, and beyond
them a thick and tall hedge-like formation of brambles and spruce blocks
entrance to the dark forest. To the north the grassy plain meets the sky at the
far horizon. A great hill pierces the skyline in the south-east.
name: Trail through Tall Grass

desc:
An utterly great hill rises east of here, seemingly impossible to scale by any
means. Here the wooded hills stand covered by animal-paths and a vast number of
conifers -- growing dense in the dales between hill-crests. From the north a
thin trail grows wider as it enters the hilly area. The trail leads further into
the hills to the south, but to the west the elevated landscape looks undisturbed
by any kind of civilization.
name: Narrow Trail by a Great Hill

desc:
Young, bright green spruce and pines climb the sides of the grassy, rolling
hills. Some of the trees can not be more than a few years old, whereas some
other are almost full-grown. A small trail winds its way through the lush dales
between the crests. The path leads into the fields west of here and to the north
the trail passes by a very tall hill which lies to the north-east. The floor of
a dim valley can be espied far down to the east.
name: Trail through Hills Shrouded in Conifers

desc:
Here the hills stand in formations creating a multitude of small dales between
the crests. Many of the hilltops looks as if they were cut off or hammered upon
by the gods ages ago -- the round, rolling hills topped by very flat surfaces. A
ridge of jagged hills is clearly visible against the eastern skyline, but to get
there one would probably have to approach them from another direction. A plateau
rises to the south, and west of here the ground lowers into the plains. This
range of hills stretches further northwards, where a winding, serpentine trail
can be seen circling the hills and dales.
name: Flat Hills

desc:
An area of flat, and somewhat rocky, lands spread out towards the south. The
barren ground is occasionally disturbed by a monolith-looking rock or small
patches of spruce. To the north a very dense and dim forest with a few very tall
trees jags the horizon, and east of here a more open area with some scattered
clumps of trees lie. To the west a high plateau can be reached by walking a
small trail which winds upwards.
name: Flat Lands

desc:
The trees grow very close here, creating a thick and moist atmosphere, almost
giving substance to the air itself. Between some bushes large, thick spiderwebs
glimmer with tiny drops of water. The forest expands to the north and east, but
to the south open and flat lands stretch out. Rocky and jagged hills stab at the
western skyline.
name: Dreary Woodlands

desc:
Thick and thorny bushes grow heavy in the small gaps between the multitude of
dark trees, the ceiling of tree-crowns being so dense only thin rifts of the sky
can be glimpsed. The dense forest spreads out to the south, but in all other
directions hills surround the woodlands. East of here a remarkably tall and
wooded hill can be seen.
name: Dreary Woodlands

desc:
A vast number of small hills -- shrouded in cloudy heather -- range from the dim
forest to the south towards the northern horizon. Patches of soft, green grass
cover the hillsides. The vague outlines of a trail winds far east of here, and
to the west a very high hilltop dominates the skyline. Climbing it would be
impossible from here, due to the jagged, sheer rockface to the west.
name: Heathery Hills

desc:
Heather covers these soft-looking and tall hills. In a small meadow between two
hills stands the stone ruins of an ancient building, moss-covered and decrepit.
To the north it is possible to climb down a somewhat steep rockface and reach
the bottom of a deep crevice, where a thin trail leads from east to west. East
of here winds a serpentine trail.
name: Heathery Hills

desc:
A deep crevice splits the land between the wooded hills to the north and the
heathery hills to the south. The narrow cleft looks almost like a deep wound in
the ground, like a long and thin rift in the landscape. A rocky trail lies at
the bottom of the crevice, leading both east and west. The northern side of the
crevice is too sheer to climb, the rockface smooth, dark rock.
name: Way through a Narrow Crevice

desc:
These hills are covered with soft, black earth. On them pines grow -- both
ancient ones and younger trees. To the east it is possible to climb a very tall
hill-top. West of here the range of hills continue without any visible
landmarks. In what looks like a broad crevice -- south of here -- a trail runs
from east to west. Scaling the sides of the land-rift would surely be lethal.
name: Pine-clad Hills

desc:
In the thick layer of black, soft earth that covers these hills tall, strong
pines have rooted ages ago. The mighty trees stand thick, but there is not much
other plantlife, apart from the moss that covers the few round boulders on the
ground. To the west, a small flat area is covered with grass -- beyond that a
tall, wooded hill. To the south and east the wooded hills roll over the land.
name: Pine-clad Hills

desc:
A thin trail winds between the hills, leading east into a narrow crevice and
south onto the plains. Spruce and an occasional pine cover most of the
hillsides, and on top of one of the hills an old and unused campsite can be
seen. North of here the pines grow more dense on the hills, making them look
even taller. To the west a field with tall, brown grass goes on towards the
horizon.
name: On a Trail through the Wooded Hills

desc:
Brown grass, more than five feet tall, grows on these fields. A small, worn
trail leads west into the grassy fields and north, winding its way through
heavily wooded hills. To the south a very tall hill stands, its northern side
being a sheer, unclimbable rockface.
name: Trail through Tall Grass

desc:
Descending from this incredibly tall hilltop would only be possible to the
south. The hillsides to the west, north and east are sheer and gives no support
for climbing them. To the south however, lies a deep and dark valley. East of
here a range of heather-shrouded hills can be spotted, and beyond them the vague
outlining of a serpentine trail. The flat area to the north is also split by a
thin trail, possibly the same one as far east of here. The northern trail bends
off to the west into the jagged hills that pierce the western skyline.
name: On a Rocky Hilltop

desc:
This valley is quite deep, its floor covered by a thick mix of moss and dark
green grass. Some lone spruce trees rise from the damp valley floor. The western
side of this bowl in the terrain is steep, and climbing it would be hard. Moving
to the east or south would be a much easier way of ascending from this silent
and secluded place, the valley-sides in those directions not at all as steep as
the western one. At the northern end of the valley, the ground rises far off
towards the sky, forming a tall hill.
name: Secluded Valley

desc:
Hills composed of brown-red rock form a massive of jagged, cracked hills devoid
of any trees or flowers. The sheer hillsides and the harsh terrain to the west
makes passage to the lower, flat hills there impossible. To the north the
entrance to a valley can be guessed, and south of here highlands stretch further
on, covered by low plants and bushes. East of here lies dim woodlands.
name: Jagged Hills

desc:
Short, worn green grass covers this large elliptic plateau, as well as a few
dark green bushes and an occasional young pine. The ground slopes downwards to
the south, into an area of low hills. East of here a range of taller hills rolls
over the landscape. To the east a swift descent upon the plains is possible.
name: Dry Plateau

desc:
The Road bends to the south and east before continuing its long journey from
Rivendell to Bree. The countryside is rather barren hereabouts with the notable
exception of a prosperous arbour of unusually tall and vital larches on one side
of the Road.
name: Bend in the Old East Road

desc:
The Road passes alongside a broad dale, with abundant trees and brush all about
you. The brush is so overgrown to the south as to effectively impede all travel
in that direction--only the dint of many hours chopping and hacking would allow
even minor progess.
name: Old East Road

desc:
The Road plods onward, its surface worn and effaced by the travails of time and
the impact of many a foot, hoof, or wheel. On either side of the road are mounds
and patches of brush and brambles, growing so hardily to the south as to
discourage all attempts to breach them there.
name: Old East Road

desc:
To the south a dense patch of weeds and brambles makes entry into the forest
seem quite impossible. To the north, however, the woodland has rather a weak and
sickly appearance, with only the most sparse undergrowth, and a scattering of
thin conifers. The road itself seems rough and unkempt.
name: Old East Road

desc:
An impenetrable natural hedgerow of gorse and brambles stands to the south of
the road, whilst to the north lies a narrow band of hills. The road itself is
rough and stony, and shows little sign of regular recent use for heavy haulage,
although it might easily be more often utilised for foot or mounted traffic.
name: Old East Road

desc:
Marching doggedly onward from east to west, the Old East Road here lies
immediately to the south of a narrow band of hills. To the other side the
carriageway is bounded by a vast tract of woodland. Some of the trees grow so
close to the road that their lowest branches reach well over the path, and an
unwary traveller on horseback might easily find himself tumbled to the ground by
these limbs.
name: Old East Road

desc:
With hilly country to the north, and a thick area of woodland to the south, a
traveller along this part of the Old East Road might find it difficult should he
seek a suitable campsite. However, judging by their appearance, the woods should
offer good hunting for those at need, for they are thick and lush, and should
support a good variety of wildlife.
name: Old East Road

desc:
The road here is littered with stones and a few tumbled boulders, which appear
to have originated in the rocky sward just north of the carriageway. To the
south you can see a thin area of woodland, the outskirts of a large forest,
which stretches away to the southeast.
name: Old East Road

desc:
Turning from west to north, the road travels through a low point in the land,
and is littered by rocks and a few scattered boulders, which have apparently
tumbled from an open area of sloping land to the northwest. To the south of the
road lies what might once have been a pasture.
name: A Bend in the Road

desc:
Rocks and stones, and even a few small boulders litter the road here, where it
travels through a shallow dip, making its way from east to south, then on
westward across the land. The ground both east and north stands a little bit
above the elevation of the road, whilst to the south and east it levels out once
more. A vast forest looms across your field of view to the north.
name: A Bend in the Road

desc:
A little way to the north of here, a rough and bloody trail beats a ragged path
through the forest. Neither have the trees and undergrowth here wholly escaped
the depredations of whatever creature rent the trampled the vegetation there.
Everywhere you look there are broken saplings and down-trodden bushes and weeds.
name: Off a Bloody Trail

desc:
Here the trail is marked with many bloodstains some of them fresh. Bending to
the north and to the west, the rough path continues through the thick woods, and
it is apparent that only creatures of massive strength could have ploughed such
a course through the forest.
name: Along a Bloody Trail

desc:
Bits of blood and gore of various ages lie everywhere here. It looks as if some
giant hand tore something limb from limb. A strange place to call home, or a
place to flee for your lives. Large, splayed feet marks lie all over the heavily
trodden ground.
name: Along a Bloody Trail

desc:
The road ploughs a steady east to west furrow across the country, as it makes
its way bewteen Rivendell and far distant Bree. To the south it is bounded by
what must once have been the lands of a smallholding. But to the north the
thoroughfare is bounded by an area which seems to have been cleared of trees
much more rapidly, and without a thought to farming.
name: Old East Road

desc:
Scattered clumps of fir and pine coexist with small patches of beech and
mountain oaks. The ground rises sharply to the east, climbing into a untold
number of hills. To the north the woods become thick and gloomy, bearing an
enigmatic menace. Silence and somnolence prevail, broken only by the distant cry
of a dark bird of omen.
name: Scattered Clumps of Trees

desc:
The very air around you seems murky and substantial, so closely the trees grow
here. Even in the daytime, twin pinpoints of light suggestive of eyes peer out
from the dim black vegetation. The land rears up in saw-edge peaks and ridges
dominating the eastern skyline.
name: Dreary Woodlands

desc:
Suddenly rising from the surroundings is a rock-tumbled hillside thickly
blanketed with evergreens. Peering through the trees, one can detect a small
strip on a trail winding here and there to the northeast. Dense primeval forest
lies all about, thick bundles of firs and pines.
name: Tree-shrouded Hillside

desc:
While in places faint and overgrown, or choked with fallen stones and trees, at
one time this path seems to have been much used. It was a path made by strong
arms and heavy feet. Here and there old trees have been cut or even broken down,
and the larger rocks have been cloven or heaved aside to make a way.
name: Serpentine Trail

desc:
Undulating from side to side in harmony with the contours of the land, the trail
skirts by a large collection of hills to the north, winding east towards rising
forests. The wayside is fairly barren, grassy and dotted with small flowering
plants; few trees are to be seen.
name: Serpentine Trail

desc:
Wide hills rise and fall above the winding trail at their feet. The hillsides
are green with plentiful grasses and a sprinkling of flowers and other small
clinging flora. A narrow but distinct way leads from the trail below winding
upward to a particularly tall summit to your north.
name: Narrow Way through the Hills

desc:
The high hilltop affords a superb view of the surrounding countryside. Most
notably you see a thin ribbon of a trail that seems to originate far to the
northeast, beyond the horizon, though you see it pass by a stone tower at the
edge of your sight. Somewhere far to the southwest, it most likely reaches the
road, although this is beyond your immediate view. Closer at hand, the land is
rocky and windswept, bereft of trees. All that grows here are some short grasses
and thorny bushes; if one plans to spend time observing the surroundings it
would be wise to account for the weather as there is nothing to shelter you from
wind, rain, or snow.
name: Atop a Panoramic Eminence

desc:
Tall pines have put their roots deep in the soil, and now guard these hills from
wind and rain. Yet in places water has cleared paths in the terrain, taking some
of the ground with it.
name: Wooded Hills

desc:
All is silent below the canopy, and no single leaf or branch moves. One could
almost expect something happening, but nothing does. The air is still and smells
of decaying fungus. The forest comes to an abrupt end at the sloping hills in
the east, but in the north it descends down without changing its shape.
name: Deep, Dank Forest

desc:
The rocks that cover the field in the east are less common here, so there is
more room for trees to put their feet on. Rather efficiently they have so done
too, the trunks are big and the branches strong. Sky cannot be seen through the
canopy, but some light enters the forest from the east.
name: Deep, Dank Forest

desc:
The forest is broken by a clearing in the north, and there are some deers there
eating grass. One of them has met the end of his life here though, body ripped
open and half-eaten. It has not yet started to decay though, but it very soon
will due to number of worms and other little beings crawling around.
name: Forest South of a Clearing

desc:
Here in the south corner of the clearing the grass slowly turns into bushes and
eventually to the dark trees that block your view to the south and east.
name: Southern Edge of a Clearing

desc:
Here the canopy is less thick, and some light throws shadows in the bushes and
plants. Birds can be heard singing happy songs in the middle of this otherwise
gloomy forest and that had to be a bouncing little rabbit that just jumped away
from your sight, or maybe it was something utterly different.
name: In a Deep Forest

desc:
Once again the sky disappears from the sight and only dark coloured branches of
the trees can be seen, like a ceiling held up by these mighty trunks that seem
to be feeding some life. Indeed, some trunks are being eaten alive by worms,
termites and whatever there might be crawling inside them.
name: Deep in a Dark Forest

desc:
In the east lies a green sloping land rising to a ridge of small hills. To the
west a set of tall trees hide the view with their grey barks that cast dark
shadows upon the land.
name: Edge of a Dark Forest

desc:
Only a few bushes of undergrowth have found space among the huge trunks and
their roots, which at some places look like snakes circling in the soil. The sky
remains unseen beyond the canopy.
name: In the Shadow of Huge Trees

desc:
All around the view is blocked by darkened trunks of trees. Branches grow only
in the upper parts of the trees, forming a natural ceiling over this temple of
trees.
name: In a Dark Forest

desc:
For some reason there is no sign that any trees at all have ever grown here.
Instead the field covered with grass, obviously feeding the few deers that live
in the area. Here and there the grass has been bent, showing where the animals
have been resting just recently.
name: Clearing in a Forest

desc:
The trees grow close to each other, letting little light enter the undergrowth.
Only a few tiny green bushes have found room to grow among the huge trunks, and
their future does not look that bright either.
name: In a Dark Forest

desc:
The already dense trunks suddenly seem to grow together, but in fact it is the
western wall of an old stone building. There are no windows on this side, bar a
small closed hole in the upper part of the wall. At the roof level the canopy
partly blocks your vision, but there definitely is something even taller than
the trees up there.
name: Forest by a Dark Tower

desc:
The cook that once might have dwelt here, has been long gone and it shows. Foul
smell fills the air as if corpses were rotting somewhere near, and the floor is
covered with dirt. Here and there there are marks of something still living
here, but it is definitely not human. Through a broken door in the east wall a
livingroom can be seen, and a little staircase leaves up near the door.
name: In an Old Kitchen

desc:
The room has not been cleared in ages, it seems. Obviously the current owner of
the tower does not mind living in a mess like this. A wooden table stands near
the northern wall, surrounded by several chairs. On the west wall there hang at
least two rabbit corpses and a bigger furry corpse on the floor below them. Next
to the corpses a doorway leads to a smelly kitchen. The door out is in the east
wall between two little windows.
name: A Dirty Livingroom

desc:
The path coming from the fields in the south turns to the east and disappears
into the forest. A small footpath continues in north west where it disappears
from the sight behind an ancient tower. West from here there is a wall with two
windows and a wooden door between them, leading probably into the tower. A small
plaque can be seen just to the left of the door.
name: Outside an Old Tower

desc:
A circling old trail comes from the north and after a rise on a hill in the
south, it disappear from the sight. Far in the north, where terrain is flat, it
seems to turn sharply to the east.
name: A Trail by a Field

desc:
A little used trail climbs up to these hills from the north and continues far to
the south. Down on the west side of the hill the fields are covered with grass
and small bushes.
name: A Trail by a Plain

desc:
Nothing has been done to keep this trail clear in ages, and trees are about to
take it over. In the north there is a break in the forest, filled with old
stumps. In the west a high tower rises high above the treetops.
name: On a Forested Trail

desc:
The forest gets more and more dense in the east. South of the road, where the
terrain slopes sharply, the forest is mixed, but in the north it is dominated by
tall and dark spruces.
name: On a Forested Trail

desc:
The trail turns north into a very dense part of forest. It looks impossible to
ride that way. In the west the road continues directly as far as the forest
allows you to see.
name: Bend in a Forested Trail

desc:
Trees have almost filled the trail here. Some of the branches are so sturdy and
low that one has to constantly keep dodging them. Dark coloured birds circle all
around, looking suspiciously at weird visitors. The forest disappears in the
east, making travelling easier that way.
name: Bend in a Forested Trail

desc:
Following the trail further east, it makes a turn northwards before entering yet
another forest like that in the west. Small hills can be seen in the south, and
far away you can see some higher ones behind the forest.
name: Trail North of a Forest

desc:
Trying to avoid the haunting trees, the trail turns north following the edge of
the forest, only to be forced to enter it there. In the west the trail crosses a
wide plain before disappearing again. The plain continues to the south all the
way to a ridge of hills that block your vision further.
name: A Turn in the Trail

desc:
An old trail coming through the plain from south makes a sharp turn to the dark
forest east of here. Several large footprints are visible in the undergrowth,
following the trail both ways. Some of them look not that old at all.
name: On a Trail

desc:
The trail twists and turns its way through the dense pine forest. The boughs of
the trees crowd over the path leaving only a small clear gap through to the sky.
A thick carpet of pine needles makes the trail soft and springy underfoot which
eases the burden on tired legs. The trail continues to the north and the west.
To the east and south the forest rises up a sharp incline from the trail.
name: Bend in a Trail

desc:
From your position amid the pines of the wooded slope, you can gaze down upon a
trail twisting its way through the woods to the west. The creatures whose feet
and hooves have beaten the path below have shied from climbing the sharp slope
from which you now survey the land. The ground drops away to the west and the
north. To the south and the east the forest thickens.
name: Overlooking a Trail

desc:
The trail passes an ominous forest to the north, the fair lands far to the west.
The trail drags on towards the east, creeping around the forest as a snake would
wrap around it's victim.
name: Small Trail

desc:
The trail seems to cringe away from the forest to the west and north. A skull
hangs from a branch nearby, leering at you as you pass its vigil. One can only
guess as to who placed the warning.
name: Small Trail

desc:
Thick and gloomy, the forest envelops you. Little light filters down from the
closely twined branches above your head, and the densely packed trunks deaden
and distort sound. Now and then a furtive rustle and cracking of branches can be
heard from somewhere deep within the trees.
name: Dark Pine Forest

desc:
Thick boughs of dense pine block out all light from above, plunging the forest
into perpetual night. The ground is soft and yielding underfoot, being composed
of decayed needles and twigs fallen from the trees over the years. Any noise
from your passage seems to cause answering rustles and shiftings from the
undergrowth, too irregular to be mere echoes.
name: Darkened Forest

desc:
The trail skirts the forest to the south here, separating it from the diseased
land to the south. As you watch your step you notice the previous rain has not
yet erased the foot prints of some large two legged creature.
name: Small Trail

desc:
A strange combination of scents fills the air along this trail. The strong scent
of pine from the forest to the north combined with the musty, putrid smell of
fungi to the west. Perhaps it is best to leave this area to the beasts.
name: Small Trail

desc:
This small east-west trail runs past a dark and foreboding forest to the south,
and a field of tall grasses to the north. Much farther to the north can be seen
the border of another forest. One can only wonder what beasts roam these woods,
at least, one can hope they can only wonder.
name: Small Trail

desc:
The trees here seem old, and tired. Their large trunks support a wide and dense
canopy, but the burden seems to have taken its toll. For under their branches
roam those beings who prefer the dark, and survive only by the grace of night.
name: Dark Dreary Forest

desc:
You have entered the edge of a thick forest, which blankets the north with dark
shadows and green and brown highlights. When it is overhead the yellow eye turns
the canopy above a brighter green but its rays do not reach the forest floor,
making the area the hunting ground of creatures of the dark.
name: Dreary Forest

desc:
Shattered trees mark the edge of the forest here. Apparently something has a
habit of hiding here and charging out to the trail to surprise the unwary
traveller. The clicking sounds of beetles can be heard from some of the broken
trees, no doubt eating the tree from within.
name: Dreary Forest

desc:
Trees here give way to the south and east, forming the border to the forest of
sorrow. East of the forest can be seen the remains of an old shack, and to the
south a small trail leading it's way across the morbid landscape.
name: Dreary Forest

desc:
The gloom of the forest has been stymied by a small trail that can be seen
running to the north and west, providing a break from the trees, and thus the
darkness that they hide below their branches. Further to the south, however, the
gloom is abundant, threatening to fill your heart.
name: Dark Forest

desc:
Large knotted trees reach their branches upward here, supporting a massive
canopy that filters the light from the air before it reaches the forest floor.
While there is a lot of activity overhead, the bottom of the forest is barren.
name: Dark Forest

desc:
What once must have been a strong set of trees has begun to droop their
branches, and an air of hopeless can be felt. West of here you can see a large
number of dead and dying trees, being eaten by some pestilence that threatens to
invade this forest.
name: Dark Forest

desc:
Deep and thick the forest blankets the land. All around you the firs stand
straight and tall, their high branches casting a shadow over all beneath them.
The dense forest has its own distinctive odour of bark and sap. Indeed a hunter
would be better to rely on a good sense of smell in this dim underworld.
name: Thick Black Forest

desc:
The dark fir trees surround you, growing high above and cutting even the strong
light of noon to a mere glimmer at the forest floor. Pine needles and cones shed
from the boughs litter the ground, decaying to form a moist layer of nutrients
for the trees from whence they came. The forest seems to thin toward the west.
name: Deep amid the Dark Pines

desc:
What used to be a part of the forest is now part of the grave. The majority of
the trees that once stood here have withered and died, the remaining trees seem
ready to share that fate. In the boughs of one of the trees can be seen a
massive horde of webs, crawling with worms. No doubt the source of this
pestilence.
name: Blighted Land

desc:
From the outside this broken down shack seemed rather mundane. Boards could be
seen lying around, and the walls had been shortened a notch or two, scarcely
tall enough to provide shelter for a hobbit. Upon closer inspection you can see
the shack has a new purpose. In the centre stands a firepit, complete with spit
for roasting. A humanoid skeleton remains tied to the spit.
name: Old Ruins

desc:
The trail bends to the north and west here, skirting a forest that sits to the
south and east. Although you havn't heard of this trail being frequented by any
of the fair races of arda, it has certainly been trampled down well by some
large humanoids on a fairly frequent basis.
name: Small Trail

desc:
If this trail gave you the creeps when it passed the forest, you should feel
like running this stretch. All around the land has been overcome with disease,
with many dead trees to the north, and to the south nothing but fungi eagerly
devouring what has been left by the ravages of disease.
name: Small Trail

desc:
The trail here splits, a short path leading north to the site of a broken down
shack. Looking to the south you can see what may have been farmland at some
point, now looking more like a fungi garden. Certainly the fate of the builders
of the shack follow that of their old fields.
name: Small Trail

desc:
This stark land bears nothing useful to the world. Tall fungi grow almost as
tall as the withered trees, often feeding on the stumps of those trees fallen
long ago. More than anything else however, it is the smell that drives you to
leave this area, for fungi do not have the most pleasant smell.
name: Blighted Land

desc:
Withered trees, shrunken from disease and the torture of wind dot the landscape
here. The fungi however seem to relish this place, growing in abundance. Their
sweet and sickly smells are quite overpowering, and probably the reason no
animal life can be seen either.
name: Blighted Land

desc:
All of the higher plant and animal forms seem to have left this place for more
hospitable areas. A few dead and withered trees can be seen, along with the
multitudes of mushrooms that litter the ground. Obviously some sort of disease
has overtaken the plant-life here.
name: Blighted Land

desc:
The trail curves to the north here, avoiding the dead land to the east. Even an
old bespectacled hobbit can see that some large humanoid has frequented this
trail, the large footprints buried in the mud. It may be wise to pass this
section of trail before night descends.
name: Small Trail

desc:
You are amidst a curious gap in the forest. The soil seems thick but the air
here has a strange tang to it and no plants or animal tracks can be seen. The
forest crowds round the clearing on the north, south and west sides. The
clearing continues to the east.
name: A Gap in the Trees

desc:
Slender trees grow amidst brush and bramble in all directions. The thin forest
is airy and light and chirpings and chitterings of small woodland animals sound
from the undergrowth. To the south and west the trees peter out and to the east
the forest becomes dark and foreboding.
name: Thin Forest

desc:
The ground rises steadily from the low forest in the north to a high ridge of
hillside in the south. To the east and west forest climbs up the foothills
toward your present location but peter out leaving this open back of high ground
exposed to the elements. The ground underfoot is rocky and hard with a few
divots of coarse grass growing amidst the boulders where they can.
name: Desolate Foothills

desc:
Shattered and splintered boughs and trunks lie bent and twisted all around.
Mosses and lichens grow over the broken surfaces showing that whatever caused
this gross damage did so in times long past. The forest recovers to the south as
it climbs up the lower reaches of a large hill. A bare ridge of higher ground
extends from the hillside to the east.
name: Broken Trees

desc:
Dim and dank, the dark trees stand silently watching you. In all directions, the
forest continues. The uncanny quiet lends the place an uneasy air, any small
sound being followed by a deep foreboding silence. Overhead the boughs of the
trees reach down for you, their twigs and branches like long gnarled fingers.
name: Gloomy Forest

desc:
The ground here lies above the level of the surrounding forest to the north and
east. To the west the land rises steeply up the flank of a ridge which protrudes
from the large hill to the south-west. The firs and pines of the forest do not
appear to grow well on the hard rock-strewn ground rising to only fifteen feet
in many cases.
name: Stunted Conifers

desc:
This narrow ridge of high ground protrudes outward from the hill to the south
and runs deep into the lowland forest to the north. The forests climb the sides
of the spur to the east and the west but do not reach to the crest, leaving a
narrow strip of bare land, covered only with hillside grass and heather.
name: A Northerly Spur

desc:
The tree cover on this north facing slope of hillside is sparse. Stunted pines
grow across the face, struggling to find purchase on the thin and rocky soil.
The slope continues upward to the south, climbing toward a summit. To the north,
you can descend into the lower forest. To the east, a spur of hillside runs
outward from the massive flank of the range.
name: Thin and Sparse Forest

desc:
The ground rises slightly to the south, the trees leaning into the slope like
massive balusters. The soil of the slope is friable and loose, making progress
uphill tiring. To the north, east and west the forest continues. To the south
the trees begin to thin out.
name: Deep amid the Pines

desc:
The slope increases to the south as it climbs up the beginnings of a large hill.
The trees grow tall in the fertile ground reaching upward to catch light on this
north facing slope. Telltale droppings scattered on the ground beside small
burrows show that woodland animals also make their home here.
name: Sloping Woods

desc:
Tall pine trees cover this steep slope. The hillside to the south rises a foot
in height for every two feet travelled making progress in that direction a
tiring climb. High above your head, the pine branches from a canopy of needles
through which dim light filters from above. The close spaced trunks deaden sound
and the forest seems almost unnaturally still and silent.
name: Tree Covered Hillside

desc:
The slope rises steeply to the south gaining a foot in height for every two
travelled. The forest clings tenaciously to the thin soil of the hillside, the
trunks angling up toward the sky. The sweet smell of pine sap hangs in the air.
To the east and west the forest continues across the slope. The hill rises to
the south and drops away to the north.
name: Steep Wooded Slope

desc:
The field slopes gently to the south toward a ridge of undulating hills and
disappears further behind a couple of higher tops. The ground is mostly filled
with grass and short bushes.
name: A Sloping Field

desc:
Trees here are not as tall as they are in the north, instead the older trees
here seem to be birch. Obviously spruces are slowly overtaking the area though.
A small trail circles at the plains northeast from here.
name: In a Young Spruce Forest

desc:
The conifers of the woodland grow tall and straight across the face of the
hillside. Over the years many pine needles have fallen to form a soft, thick
blanket on the ground. The forest continues in all directions, though to the
south, the trees begin to thin out as the slope increases.
name: High above a Trail

desc:
The ground rises steeply to the south, climbing up the flanks of the range of
hills cutting across the land. Tall and straight conifers grow all around,
rising proudly from the springy bed of fallen pine needles which lies thick
around their bases. Any disturbance of the pine needles releases the thick aroma
of moist earth and wood sap into the air.
name: Forested Slope

desc:
Trees around you are so huge and tall that they seem to be disappearing in the
clouds above you. Through the trunks you see a more open area in the north, but
everywhere else the forest continues. Luckily the forest is not very dense, so
moving here is not a problem.
name: Northern Edge of a Forest

desc:
Here the tall trees that tower high above ground in the south have not blocked
sun from warming the ground, letting some grass grow in the soil. Following the
border of the forest to the east, there seems to be a path going towards a huge
pine.
name: A Grassy Field

desc:
The the path coming from the forest behind a tall pine in the east disappears
here. If it ever continued west, it is now overgrown with grass. In the south
there is a forest of tall trees.
name: Path by a Grassy Field

desc:
The trees have grown very straight here, and they have just a few branches in
their lower part making climbing to them rather hard. A couple of squirrels run
up and down the trees chasing each other. The soil is sandy here, only in some
places grass grows on it.
name: In a Forest

desc:
An about ten yards wide opening runs from north-west to south-east here between
the two forests. While the forest far in the east is strictly spruce, the other
forest right on the west side of the path is mainly pine. In the south there is
a ruin of an old fortification, upon which a high tower rises.
name: Path between Two Forests

desc:
The path circles through the forest and right next to the wall of a tower south
of here. It then seems to make a turn behind the corner in east and disappears
from the sight. There is a thick spruce forest north of here.
name: Bend in a Forest Path

desc:
The forest is much lighter here than in north where you cannot see anything more
than a few yards away. Small animals have also found this light forest better
place to live in. South of here there is a wide opening in the forest and a
crossroad of three paths.
name: Southern Edge of a Spruce Forest

desc:
All around you there are rotten stumps, now housing colonies of termites and
small worms. The clearing continues further in the north and in the south it
leads to a trail.
name: A Break in the Forest

desc:
The trees are young and short here, indicating that the area has not always been
so densely forested. Indeed, the soil here is so good it might have been used as
a field sometime in the past. But in the coming years it will become as dark as
the older forest north of here.
name: Low Forest

desc:
A wide plain opens up here, bordered by a dark forest in the west and far in the
east. A little trail runs over the plain in the southeast, only to yet again
disappear in the frightening forest.
name: Plain beside the Gloomy Forest

desc:
The most visible landmark here is a gigantic ant hill, its top reaching high
above. It must have taken centuries for the colony of ants to build this hill,
even with the great number of them.
name: Ant Hill in the Dreary Forest

desc:
The forest borders a small clearing west of you, surrounding it like tall city
walls. Only some grass grows on the ground, most of the soil being covered by
fallen spruce needles. A tiny path circles at your feet to the east, filled with
busy ants.
name: Edge of the Dreary Forest

desc:
You are in a little clearing, surrounded by tall, dark green spruces in east and
west. Some of the trees that have grown here in the past have been cut down, and
the rather dry soil of this location has not allowed new trees to grow up.
name: Opening in the Forest

desc:
Sharp needles make moving in the forest difficult. The twigs almost seem to be
reaching down for prey from the darkness above. The forest continues dark as
ever in the west, where as in other directions it is not so thick.
name: Dark Spruce Forest

desc:
The trees allow no light to enter the undergrowth, causing an uninviting and
eerie darkness. There grows nothing green in the soil, and only animals living
here show nothing of themselves but an eye or two in the darkness around you.
name: Deep in a Dark Spruce Forest

desc:
The ancient pine stands here like a king, surrounded by smaller trees. The path
makes a sharp turn around the trunk of the tree and continues south and west. Up
in the tree there is a bird nest between two branches.
name: At an Old Pine

desc:
Probably the remains of an old building, huge stones have broken the forest into
little clearings. No clear outline of the building can be seen, but it seems to
have been rather big, since some of the stones are several feet in diameter.
name: A Forest Field

desc:
The trees are rather short here compared to rest of the forest. Up above the
treetops a ruined tower rises to guard the woods around it. There is a slight
decline in the terrain to the east, leading to a ruined field.
name: Forest South of a Tower

desc:
Old moss covered stones around the field suggest that it was once protected from
wild animals by a fence. Now, there seems to be nothing to protect. Thick
undergrowth and little bushes have filled the field.
name: An Old Farm Field

desc:
A few trees have been just recently torn from their roots by god knows what.
Beside the trunks there are tracks allright, but it is very hard to tell if this
has been done by many large beings or one really big.
name: At Some Fallen Trees

desc:
Though fertile, the soil is too rocky for more than grass and small bushes to
grow. In the south, where the terrain meets the root of a hill range, the rocks
disappear and are replaced by thick bushes that seem to thrive in the slope.
Through a strangely direct row of trees, a small trail can be seen in the east.
name: Ancient Fields

desc:
These undulating hills are covered with thick bushes everywhere, and the slopes
come as a surprise because they cannot be seen before getting to them. It is
also just as hard to see what lurks in the undergrowth.
name: Bushy Hills

desc:
The last sad remains of a dry stone wall divide this land from the Old East
Road, which sweeps past its western and northern borders. Now only scattered
piles of tumbled rocks and stones remain, and a lot of these have rolled well
within the borders of the field, suggesting that the walls were brought down by
brute force. The ground itself is a sea of weeds and light brush, but the dirt
below shows signs that it was once ploughed and tended, for even today long,
deep furrows still scar the earth, though the crops once planted have been
choked out by the wild vegetation.
name: Fallow Land beside the Road

desc:
Water cascades lightly over the edge of a natural weir, which restricts the
outflow of a small lake just north of here. Spreading around this shallow basin,
the stream is quite shallow at this point, and its current is very gentle as it
flows off to the east, its way south blocked here by a rising hillside.
name: A Shallow Stream below the Weir

desc:
A small stone building once stood here. What remains now is mostly in ruins, but
the innermost room is still partially intact. However, brief inspection appears
to indicate a long period of abandonment, judging by the advanced state of
decay. The floor is cracked in places, the walls have crumbled down, and no roof
covers the structure.
name: At a Ruined Guardhouse

desc:
A small hill lies just north of here, and it must be from its crest that so many
boulders and stones have toppled. Close inspection suggests that many, or
perhaps most of these are in fact tooled, and it seems likely that they were
once a part of the ruined structure which sits atop the hill. But wherever they
came from, they have wreaked havoc on the plant life of this area. A few mighty
tree trunks lie flat amid the rocks, and only a single living tree survives.
name: Tumbled Boulders

desc:
The forest comes to an abrupt ending just south of here, as if someone had
deliberately razed the trees to the ground. And beyond a wide, open area lies
the unmistakable expanse of a road, cutting its way from east to west across the
land. To the north the woods peter out more gradually, as they enter upon a
region of tumbled rocks and mighty boulders.
name: At the Edge of the Woods

desc:
A broad band of the forest has been razed to the ground, leaving a field of
broken stumps, stretching south toward the road. Some of the trees have been
felled by axe or saw, but others have been burned down along with the under-
growth. It is apparent that this was done quite some time ago, for thin grass
and a few small weeds are already peaking up through the ruined soil, and no ash
and cinders are to be seen, even on the biggest of the burned out stumps.
name: A Field of Stumps

desc:
Oaks and elms, ancient and hoary stand proud amid a proliferation of younger
firs and pines. The trees grow close together, and beneath them thrives a dense
undergrowth of bushes, wildflowers and weeds. The only strange thing about this
forest is how abruptly it seems to end. Whilst the road to the south offers
partial explanation, there is little to suggest why the foliage should not
continue unabated to the east.
name: At the Edge of the Forest

desc:
Rising sharply to the west, the land merges with a narrow ridge of hills. To the
north it rises more gradually, where it draws close to a forest. South and east,
a wide road passes by, travelling through a shallow dip in the countryside.
Rocks and stones litter the ground here, and no small number of these must
tumble regularly toward the thoroughfare.
name: A Rocky Incline

desc:
The land here forms what is almost a peak, dropping off gently in every
direction. Westward it enters a broad gully, whilst to the south it slopes off
toward a rocky incline, which marks the eastern end of a narrow ridge of hills.
To the north and east the gradient is more gentle, and in these directions the
woodland itself stretches out before you.
name: A Wooded Incline

desc:
Spruce and tall, rather spindly firs stand in scattered clusters, with wide
areas comprised of little but brush and scrub growing between them. Each
individual knot of trees seems to crowd together and the ground between them
looks as though it might often have been beaten into rough trails, for even now
there is a discernible break in the vegetation heading from the gully to the
west, north toward another break in the forest.
name: Scattered Woods

desc:
The rough trail comes to an abrupt end here, as it enters a wide clearing in the
forest. The nearest trees describe an almost perfect circle about the place, and
at the very centre of this circle a pit has been dug. In this pit lie the bones
of many creatures, large and small, as well as the charred remains of logs and
flesh. A pronounced stench of death and seared meat still pervades the air, and
it seems that no breeze could fully cleanse the reek.
name: The Roasting Pit

desc:
The trees here are rather thin, and only some twisted shrubs and a few rank and
sickly weeds serve for undergrowth. The entire area here appears to have been
visited by frequent denuding, as if some large and uncaring creature chose on
occasion to beat a path through this part of the forest. Not far to the west
there is a complete break in the woodland, where a deep and rocky channel cuts
from north to south.
name: A Broken Forest

desc:
Pines and fir trees predominate in this part of the woodland, their heavy trunks
reaching high into the sky. Bushes and shrubs proliferate, making a dense
undergrowth, which is probably home to innumerable small creatures, but there is
little sign of larger game. But just west of here is an abrupt break in the
vegetation, where a deep gully cuts across the land.
name: Deep in the Woods

desc:
Fed by the waters of the stream to the east, the ground here is rich, and
supports a variety of bushes and small plants. Standing high enough above the
level of the water, it is not at all marshy, but rather a thick, rich loam. Tall
pines and firs, and a number of massive deciduous trees all thrive here, and a
dense carpet of grass and weeds covers the ground.
name: A Lush Area of Woodland

desc:
The steep slope to the west drops down into the woodland surrounding you. Pine
trees grow across the foot of the slope together with many bushes and shrubs. To
the north, somewhere out of sight, a stream can be heard splashing and gurgling
its course. To the east and south the forest thickens.
name: Foot of the Hill

desc:
The swift running river from the north bends sharply around a large rock in its
course, racing onward to the east with many a swirl and splash. The large rock
may have tumbled down from the steep slope, rising sharply upward immediately to
the west of the river. The brow of the slope frowns down upon you from several
hundred feet above your head.
name: Bend in the Creek

desc:
Two small streams merge here, one flowing swiftly down from the north, the
second tumbling into the flow from the west. Both streams run in valleys cut
into the steep hillside to the north and west. Thick grass grows over the banks
of the stream as it continues its rapid journey to the south. To the east, a
forest begins several hundred yards distant.
name: A Fork in the Stream

desc:
Rushing downhill from the west, a swiftly flowing stream tumbles into a small
lake to the east. To then north and south, trees crowd over the stream, growing
almost down to the waters edge. The stream has undercut banks several feet deep
and grasses and roots hang out over the watercourse.
name: Tumbling Stream

desc:
A small lake spreads blackly out around you. To the east, many reeds and rushes
can be seen growing in the still water of the lake. To the west a stream feeds
the lake with fresh water, its splashing and tinkling can be heard echoing out
over the lake surface. A dark stand of trees grows along the northerly edge of
the lake whilst to the south, excess water from the lake appears to spill
through a gap in the trees.
name: On a Small Lake

desc:
Many branches and twigs have been washed down into the lake to the north by the
streams feeding it. These have become stuck at this outfall and over the years
have gradually built up a weir over which the lake now empties its excess water.
Trees grow down to the water's edge to the east and the west and it may well be
their roots which have started the choke of debris from which you now gaze.
name: A Natural Weir

desc:
All around, a thick stand of pine trees sprouts forth from the moist earth. The
chirpings of woodland birds can be heard from the branches above. The ground
underfoot is springy, the thick moss and tightly packed heather growing around
the feet of the tree trunks.
name: Copse of Pine

desc:
Crooked, twisted trees shut out any light from above, lending the forest a dark
and sombre atmosphere. No colours brighten the drab undergrowth and in all
directions, dim grey trees stretch away. From somewhere to the south, running
water can be heard, tinkling and splashing beyond sight.
name: Dark and Gloomy Forest

desc:
The forest rises dark and sombre all around. Nary a chink of light passes from
above the black foliage of the closely packed boughs. The dark shadows play
tricks upon your eyes, the dark branches seeming to writhe and twist toward you
with their long fingers of crooked twigs. An evil presence hangs over these
woods.
name: Dark Dense Forest

desc:
Dim, dark boughs hang around you. The forest of the trollshaws seems unkind
toward strangers and it seems as if the trees almost jostle one another in their
attempt to enclose you and impede progress. High above you the sky must gaze
down, but the dark foliage blocks out even a glimpse of the sun or stars.
name: Dark Forest

desc:
The tree roots stick up from the damp earth underfoot. Their surfaces are slippy
with moisture and footsteps on the earth between causes water to well up from
the ground. The trees seem to thrive in these damp conditions and many varieties
of deciduous and coniferous trees grow together in a mish-mash of shapes and
sizes.
name: Damp Woodland

desc:
Many trees grow on the south-east facing slope. The soil seems thick and fertile
and the hill and cliffs to the north-west provide partial shelter from the
bitter north winds. To the south and east the land drops away to the lower
forests of the trollshaws.
name: Tree-covered Hillside

desc:
The trees grow high up the sheltered southern face of the hillside. Through gaps
in the forest, the trollshaws can be seen stretching out for miles to the south
and the east. To the west the lands drops down into a steep-sided valley. To the
north the trees peter out as the hillside climbs ever upward.
name: Steep Woodland

desc:
An almost sheer vertical face of cliff looms over you to the west. To gaze upon
its summit requires you to lean back and crane your neck as you gaze upward. The
trees of the surrounding forest to the north, south and east grow almost to the
foot of the cliff, with only piles of fallen rock and rubble separating the two.
name: Below a Cliff

desc:
High above you, a steep cliff peers down from the broad flank of the hillside to
the west. Many rocks and boulders have fallen from the cliff, eroded from the
living rock by the ravages of the wind, rain and ice. To the north, east and the
south, forest grows on the lower slopes of the hillside.
name: Base of the Cliff

desc:
This is the highest point at the eastern end of the range of hills curving deep
into the heart of the trollshaws. Several miles to the west, a higher peak can
be spied, its lofty summit looking down even on this high peak. Immediately to
the east, the hillside drops away down a dizzying cliff to the forest far below.
name: Brow of a Hill

desc:
Looking over the cliff edge to the east is a giddying experience. The ground
drops away nigh vertically for hundreds of feet. Far below, the treetops of the
forest sway in the breeze. To the south the hill climbs toward its summit and to
the north you can travel down and out upon a long spur of high ground.
name: Above a High Cliff

desc:
Several yards to the west a stream races southward, swirling and splashing down
over the rocks of the streambed. The tinkling, splashing sound of the water
fills the air. At the sides of the stream, grass grows thickly in the moist
fertile ground. To the west and north, high hillside rises upward from your
vantage point. To the east, dark pines grow thick and black.
name: Swiftly Flowing Stream

desc:
A small stream tumbles southward down the hillside. The tinkling and gurgling of
water splashing down over the rocks breaks the otherwise enveloping silence of
the woods. Many clumps of green mosses and rushes grow in the moist earth beside
the stream.
name: Burbling Brook

desc:
A small brook wells up from the broken rock of the hillside and trickles down
the slope to the south. Over the years the stream has cut into the hillside and
clumps of moss and heather grow in the sheltered niche cut by the stream. The
hillside rises to the south west and continues along the flank to the east.
name: At the Head of a Stream

desc:
The loose, friable rock of the slope makes progress across this terrain
difficult. This exposed flank is bounded by a steep-sided gully to the west.
From your present position, the bottom of the gully cannot be seen. To the east
the land rises up a shallow ridge. Sparse woodland covers the lower slopes to
the north.
name: Slope above the Trees

desc:
A few yards from you, a small waterfall tumbles down from the hillside to the
west. The splashing of the water on the rocks of the streambed causes a mist of
fine water droplets to hang in the air making exposed flesh and clothing damp.
Several tiny fish dart around in the pools by the fall.
name: Beside a Waterfall

desc:
This high knuckle of hillside stands sentinel over the low ground to the north
and east. The hillside drops steeply down from your present location, downward
into a river gouged valley to the north and east. To the south and west you can
continue along the ridge of high ground.
name: High on a Spur

desc:
Nestled below the hill summit to the north-west, this small clump of trees
flourishes in the natural protection from the harsh northerly winds. Here and
there other shrubs or a small pine grows amidst the birch and larch trees which
make most of the stand. The ground slopes downward to the south and the east.
name: Lightly Wooded Hills

desc:
A small stream trickles forth from a cleft in the hillside. The stream dances
and splashes away to the east, tumbling down the hillside as it races downhill.
A valley cuts into the hillside to the east and spurs of hill encompass the cut
to the north-east and south-east sides.
name: At the Stream Source

desc:
Far below you the green forests of the trollshaws stretch away to the east. A
small river can be seen cutting its way through the trees as it winds its way
south-east. Any movements on the loose rock of this lofty perch cause small
stones and rocks to clatter away down the slope, giving away your presence.
name: Overlooking the Valley

desc:
A sheer cliff of hard, grey stone looms above you to the north, marking the end
of a vast ridge, which cuts across the forest from northwest to southeast. The
land climbs away from here, both east and west, and this rocky gully makes a
gentle descent to the south. Boulders and smaller rubble are strewn all about
you, as if this gully were perhaps once a part of the ridge, blasted into small
pieces by some unknown force. Loose rocks and scree make both walls unappealing
prospects for exit, especially as they are almost vertical at this point.
name: In a Deep Gully

desc:
Climbing gently to the north, the gully stretches out before you, its bottom a
barren sea of tumbled rocks and boulders. To the south the rocky debris gives
way once more to the endless forest, which also rears above you to the east and
west, beyond the walls of this wide, stony trench. Both sides of the chasm are
very steep and covered in loose scree, but whilst the eastern one is almost
vertical, the western one has a shallower grade, and it is probably possible to
scramble out in that direction.
name: In a Deep Gully

desc:
A wide trench cuts through the forest from north to south. For most of its
length it is quite bare of vegetation, a bed of rocks and boulders, smaller
stones and sharp scree. But here the woods have reclaimed part of the gully, and
several small stands of pines and firs cling to life in the rocky ground.
name: Wooded Gully

desc:
Running almost due north to south, this deep gully cuts a long wound through the
forest, with almost no vegetation clinging to life in its rocky bottom. No river
bed, it is something of a mystery what could have carved this stony chasm into
the land. Its walls here are quite gently sloped, and the trench floor itself
rises to both the north and south.
name: A Deep Depression

desc:
Thick forests grow to either side of this long trench, which stretches off into
the distance, to the north. But here there is no vegetation at all, the entire
area being a barren sea of rocks and stones, in which not even a few tiny weeds
can be seen. East and west the walls of the gully rise gently toward the
woodland. To the south the land also climbs steadily, as it reaches the lower
slopes of a ridge of hills.
name: A Rising Depression

desc:
Already the hills have begun to descend toward the lower land to the east. A
steeper southern slope leads down towards the Old East Road. To the north rather
a steep grade offers access to a deep gully, which stretches due north for a
considerable distance, cutting the forest in two.
name: Toward the End of a Hilly Ridge

desc:
Covered in short furry grass like the back of a sheep, the hills carry on in the
north and descend down to a north-south trail in the east. In the west the view
is blocked by pines which form almost a natural wall on the hill.
name: Grassy Hills

desc:
Ascending slowly from the lowlands, these widely sloping hills are blanketed
with a lush carpet of grasses. Strewn in bunches are delicate clusters of tawny
flowers raising their faces to the sky from among the grassbeds. From here you
can descend the gradual slope the the trail below, climb up towards a
particularly high and wide hill, or merely remain here a bit and enjoy this
area's pastoral simplicity.
name: Broadly Sloping Hills

desc:
Slithering through the hills, this small but well-defined trail offers a
comfortable means of travel unlike the wilderness to either side. Bordering the
trail are tufts of pale grass and struggling patches of heather and furze. You
can follow the trail either to the east or south, or ascend into the nearby
hills.
name: Serpentine Trail

desc:
The trail winds sinuously down from the hills to the north into scattered brush
intermingled with stands of varied evergreens. The undergrowth borders close
upon the trail in sparse tangled patches, but there are few trees to muddle the
horizon. Looming to the east is a series of uplands, while the trail meanders to
the north and west.
name: Serpentine Trail

desc:
A large expanse of heath borders the trail thickening into some woodlands to
your east. The area is thickly grown with ling and broom and cornel, exuding a
mild fresh scent. A few small knots of dwarf pines and blue-green firs are
scattered about, more prominent as one nears the woodlands. To the south the
earth rises so sharply as to deny all access via this route.
name: A Tumbled Heathland

desc:
The land rises suddenly into jagged ridges of rock to which cling spindly
wind-battered pines. Hardy grasses and lichen also cover the fractured rocks and
boulders. The land slopes downward to the west overlooking forests and beyond
that the landscape rises into more hills.
name: An Area of Precarious Badlands

desc:
The land is less rough here, and slopes up to a large rocky hill that affords a
good view of the Old East Road below you. The landscape is endowed with a thick
blanket of underbrush from which rises a number of pines and spruce. The
vegetation suddenly ends at a sheer dropoff of a sort that no sane man would
dare to climb.
name: Atop the Forested Uplands

desc:
Your surroundings defy all attempts to categorise or define them; divergence,
discordance, and mutation prevail here. The land rises and falls without
pattern, sometimes covered in snarled tanglings of bramble or heath, in other
places covered with tumblings of fractured rock with pale tufts of grass
emerging from cracks and fissures. Various trees sprawl about, individually or
in small stands, their limbs tangled and askew. While progess in any direction
is difficult, the land becomes calmer and more even to the south and east.
name: Chaotic Woodlands

desc:
Trees cling precariously to scree-covered slopes or nestle in small niches in
the rising rockfaces. It appears that with skill one could scale the jagged
ridge of tumbled rock. All about you the land seems twisted and aimlessly
constructed.
name: Steeply Sloping Woodlands

desc:
Tumbles of rocks are piled upon each other in a random and haphazard fashion;
trees sprout from them without form or regularity, their branches swept into odd
directions and shapes by the relentless wind. The road and surrounding lands
spread out to your south--you might descend the steep slope with care and skill.
name: Chaotic Highlands

desc:
The land juts up into fractured peaks and promontories. Piles of broken rock lie
scattered about, and rifts and fissures abound; it would be best to tread
carefully here. A few trees grow in cracks and shelters, but they are far from
abundant. To the south the land becomes even more precarious; the land above you
rises high above all else, affording an excellent vista.
name: Fractured Peaks

desc:
Misty and gloomy, something about this area sends a uncomfortable prickling
running along your spine. Certainly it is dark enough, the trees and brush are
vague indistinguishable blobs even in the daytime.
name: Darkened Woodlands

desc:
The uplands loom dim and near to the southeast, towering over the scattered line
of trees that thickens to your south. The earth is soft and moist, supporting
hearty patches of brush, and a few handfuls of variegated wildflowers quivering
colourfully in the breeze. The woods open up to the west revealing a pathway, to
the east there is a V-shaped indentation cleft into the steeply rising hills.
name: Forested Borderlands

desc:
This sandy mire supports little life, but on small mounds emerging from the
muck, scraggled, twisted spruces raise sparsely-needled boughs toward the
firmanent, doggedly persisting in this unforgiving soil. There is a distinct
smell of ordure and rot--the water is darkly mottled with decaying vegetation
and other organics. Southwards the land ascends slowly towards some ragged
uplands jutting above the horizon.
name: Scattered Spruce among a Mire

desc:
The ground is wet, and in some places little ponds have formed to keep the water
on surface. Small streams seem to slowly carry rest of it eastwards. The trees
are mostly spruce, with occassional patches of short birches.
name: In a Swampy Forest

desc:
Tall and straight pines surround you with their trunks and branches that float
on the ground before diving deeper in the soil. Here the ground is still quite
dry, but in the south-east it turns more wet.
name: Below Great Pines

desc:
The trail makes its way north-east and south-west, changing the course to pass
the few obstacles on the way. Here it seems to follow the edge of the forest in
the east and in the south, until further north it disappears on a hill.
name: On a Bending Trail

desc:
The trail bends like a snake around the trees and bushes, looking for an easy
way to get to the end, but no end can be seen as far as the view is clear. In
the south it disappears behind trees, and in the east it does not get far before
another turn north.
name: On a Bending Trail

desc:
An almost unnoticeable rise in the terrain from the north keeps the grass drier
here, almost ideal for a little field. But of course no one lives here to farm
it, and it shall remain as it is until overgrown by the great forest of which
the huge trees in the west are a good example.
name: Outside a Dark Forest

desc:
This little valley seems to be where the pouring rain eventually ends up from
the forest west of here and the hills in the east. It is not quite a swamp yet,
but one could easily imagine in turning into one in the future, once the dry
seasons are no longer enough for the grass to grow up.
name: Wet Plains

desc:
The forest is getting less thick in the south, revealing a little trail rising
up and down in the ravines. The branches of the trees, which mostly seem to be
evergreen in this part, have been cut in the lower parts by force. Probably a
sign of some animal marking its territory.
name: Near a Forest Trail

desc:
The trail bends northeast where it continues until the hills seen far in the
distance. On the west side of the bend, a row of about twenty trees have grown
funnily in a direct north-south course. Another range of hills start nearby in
the south, and the trail turns to progress around it.
name: On a Bending Trail

desc:
The trail climbs down in the south, where it continues to follow the edge of the
hills, and on the east side it is bordered by a forest, mostly pine.
name: Trail between Forest and Hills

desc:
The sandy trail follows the edge of the green hills on their east side. The
trail is separated by a small region of thornbushes from the spruce forest in
the east, where the ground gets wet and in places a bit swampy.
name: On a Trail

desc:
A vaguely distinguishable trail meanders north-south through a wide expanse of
pale sand flats, interrupted only rarely by various tufts of dark-green ribbon
grass. The land slopes gradually upwards to lush grassy hills to your west and
to the east descends into a sickly mire.
name: A Sandy Trail

desc:
This slightly sandy trail veers west, avoiding the lightly forested regions to
the south. Beyond the forest the land can be seen to rise into some craggy and
desolate uplands to the east and south. Aside from a few wan flowers by the
wayside, the area seems forlorn and abandoned.
name: Serpentine Trail

desc:
You find yourself in an intermediate area of pine and cedar woodlands, fading
slowly into brush and heath to the northwest, darkening into heavier forests to
the southeast. The trees grow in compact stands, scattered patchwork amidst a
rich variety of scrub, heath, and creeping bramble. As with its surroundings,
this area is ominously quiet.
name: Mixed Pine and Cedar

desc:
Tall boughs interweave to form a dense canopy that blocks out most of the
sunlight. The forest floor exudes a moist, fungal odor; scattered about are
fiery-capped mushrooms, encrustations of brain-coral fungus, and creeping slime
molds. Northwest the forest fades into mixed trees and heathland, while to the
southeast tall uplands climb skyward. It appears that with skill and luck one
could clamber up a nearby rockface.
name: Some Rough and Dreary Woodlands

desc:
Wind-swept pines send their roots deep into to stony soil, their branches bent
back by the prevailing wind. The land swells and dips in steep jagged hills and
ridges, most notably to your east where the ground has been thrust upward
without rhyme or reason.
name: Wooded Highlands

desc:
Despite the rocky, uneven terrain, tall pines grow strong and closely packed,
darkening the forest floor. The dimness, coupled with the stony earth allows
little growth underneath the canopy. Tiny rock-chips crackle underfoot and
render travel a slow and slippery affair.
name: Heavily-timbered Badlands

desc:
Twisted pines grow closely on rough scree-covered slopes, preventing most
illumination from penetrating here. Indeed, the footing is precarious here,
progress is limited to stumbling and sliding along talus and debris. Below you
the road dips and curves along a wooded area.
name: Badlands in a Dusky Forest

desc:
A large belt of larches prosper here, their tall branches straining skyward in
open competition with the more predominant pines and firs. The undergrowth is
lush and fecund, covering most of the earth with a carpet of greenery. Scattered
amidst the green are bright clusters of purple rhododendrons, exuding a delicate
perfume that rises and mingles with the sweet resinous odor wafted into the
breeze by the proud larches. A single lonely bird pierces the silence with a
melancholy tune.
name: A Mingling of Deciduous and Cone-Bearing Trees

desc:
This road appears to have been not so much as built but instead blasted into
existence by some strange power. The surface is black and smooth, with a glassy
obsidian-like quality. Still the road gives sufficient traction to allow easy
progress along its confines. The roadside is barren and still bears signs of
whatever magics produced this trail--patches of blackened turf and skeletal
tree-boles.
name: Blasted Trail

desc:
The landscape here is desolate, unlike the heavily forested lands to your north.
The dun-coloured soil still manages to support a few patches of heather and some
withered trees stand morosely scattered by the roadside. The land is quiet and
apparently devoid of fauna. Perhaps if a hungry traveller was in need of
sustenance, they could hunt in the woods above the winding road.
name: Barren Region near a Forest

desc:
At some recent juncture a fire swept through this area, leaving blackened stumps
and contorted boles in its wake. The process of renewal has begun however; a
fresh carpet of grass has sprung up in the enriched soil. Also thriving in the
mineral-laden earth is a variety of culinary herbs; hooded morels lie in fresh
rows ready for the picking. A thin crevasse sidles along to the northwest
thwarting any progress in this direction. Southward the narrow ribbon of the
Road can be faintly discerned.
name: Burned-out Clearing

desc:
Tall larches predominate here, their slender forms and sparse, feathery foliage
of light green contrasting with the darker, stubbier pines hereabouts.
Underneath the trees' delicate green tracery of needles and tall reddish brown
trunks, small plants grow amidst a carpet of matted needles. The songs of birds
echo lightly through the trees, and you espy a large nest in one of the nearby
trees; evidently the tamaracks are a favourite nesting spot.
name: A Belt of Larches

desc:
The forest descends into a broadly sloping valley, well-favoured with
alternating patches of pine, mountain ash, and small sweetly smelling meadows of
wild rye grass sprinkled with variegated wildflowers. Some type of small
subterranean-dwelling rodents have endeavoured to dig numerous burrows in the
grass; occasionaly the head of one of these recluse creatures pops up to monitor
you.
name: A Large Dale

desc:
The landscape is somewhat barren, with patches of scrub and grasses here and
there with many bare spots inbetween. A fair number of conifers subsist in the
unforgiving soil, their branches scraggly and sparsely-needled. The Road passes
to the south.
name: Scraggly Conifers

desc:
On the very edge of the road the woodland becomes quite thin, and the rocky
ground seems to discourage the undergrowth which flourishes elsewhere in the
forest. The trees here are mostly conifers, and no small number of these seem to
be in decidedly ill health, their branches thin and weak.
name: Between Forest and Roadway

desc:
Climbing above the treeline, this narrow ridge stands above the forest, which
sprawls out west and north of here. To the south it offers a commanding view of
the Old East Road, which cuts its way across the land from east to west,
travelling between the distant settlements of Bree and Rivendell.
name: The Rising Hills

desc:
Rising out of the forest to the north, and looming above the Old East Road to
the south, this narrow ridge of hills stretches out both east and west from
here. The cruel north winds must often flay this unprotected area, and only the
barest covering of topsoil remains. It is therefore no surprise to see that only
a few stunted patches of heather manage to cling to life here.
name: Atop a Narrow Ridge of Hills

desc:
The land here slopes gently down toward a fold in the land not far to the east,
but the gradient is no so steep as to have eroded the soil, and a rich variety
of bushes, shrubs and wildflowers comprise the undergrowth. Massive pines and
firs, and a few ancient birches and oak trees surround you.
name: In a Thick Forest

desc:
The forest here is composed evenly of tall pine trees, and shorter, bushy fir
trees. The ground is quite bare, with pine needles softening the sound of your
tread. The forest seens to get darker to the east, while staying about the same
to the north. To the west the woods seem lighter, whilst a ridge of hills rises
to the south.
name: Amid the Towering Pines

desc:
To the south the forest seems fairly sparse, but here the trees grow close
together, pines and firs mixing with a few deciduous species to form a rich,
mixed forest. A variety of bushes and shrubs, mixed with weeds and a few tiny
wildflowers make a dense undergrowth, which makes travel through this area
rather more difficult, but at the same time adds to the wholesome air of this
part of the woodland.
name: Mixed Forest

desc:
This area transitions from some sparsely forested areas into a lowly rising
meadow to the north. A few pines and firs populate the area, but they are
neither prolific nor prosperous. Such scattered brush and herbs that prefer this
limey soil manage fairly well here.
name: Mixed Forest

desc:
Clusters of pines and firs with an occasional larch grow between small grassy
meadows spotted with small flowers. All about are trees, not so thick as to
block out the sky yet prevalent enough to dominate all fields of view.
name: Mingled Conifers

desc:
A narrow ravine winds alongside this freshly-scented zone of trees amid small
patches of furze, heather, and bramble accentuated with tiny ground-clinging
flowers. In every direction you see trees and more trees, although at some point
across the ravine the trees become thin and feeble.
name: Ravine along the Forest

desc:
Jutting from the cracked, lifeless earth is a tall ebony monolith that seems to
reach up and pierce the sky. It looks as if it were not built so much as it was
summoned from the bowels of the earth. The surface of the obelisk is covered
with oddly formed runes that writhe and pulsate, emanating a sallow, unholy
light. A small obsidian altar is beside it, square with a bowl-shaped
indentation coated with some brownish-red powder.
name: At a Forbidding Obelisk

desc:
The glossy black trail suddenly winds to the east, where you espy a looming
black monolith, limned by a sallow glow. Even from this distance, it is
intuitively obvious that the obelisk is the source of ancient evil, embued with
a malevolent power. Beyond the obelisk the land decays into a series of mud
flats; a strange redolence reminiscent of rotten eggs eminates from that
direction.
name: Blasted Trail

desc:
The closely packed trees blanket a steeply rising slope. The ground is veritably
cloaked with sedge, bramble, and ivy. Soft mutterings and occasional quiverings
in the undergrowth imply the presence of some small animal life. A cliff rises
almost vertically to the east and only at a slightly smaller gradient to the
north.
name: A Densely Forested Incline

desc:
Fir trees grow profusely, exuding a deep, resinous odor that elicits twinges in
your nostrils. Brush and brambles wind about the trees, slowing any travel.
Close to the north a wide sawtooth ridge rises above the horizon.
name: A Preponderance of Firs

desc:
Constrained on either side by winding crevasses, the trail proceeds north and
south. A palpable sense of evil radiates from the north, though the source or
reason is unclear. Something tells you that you should be well prepared if you
wish to return from there alive.
name: Blasted Trail

desc:
Weaving its way along a precipice, a small goat-trail allows progress along the
jagged ridge, rising eventually to a peak north of you, or descending rapidly
downwards navigating the hillside in long cutbacks back and forth along the only
available pathways downward. The rocky land is mainly barren, but as usual with
this area, a few scraggly evergreens fight for life in any site with a modicum
of shelter from the chill mountain winds.
name: A Precipitous Height

desc:
The jagged hills crest to a dizzying height here; indeed it seems as if one has
reached the highest point of the Pennath Tar. The altitude affords a mighty
vista overlooking the surrounding lands. To the south is a thin winding ribbon
which must be the Old East Road curving and rising in unison with the rolling
landscape. All about the hills the lands are blanketed in rich forests; on and
on and on without visible end. A strange odour of rotting eggs wafts along the
breeze, evidently from the immediate east. Peering downward you notice an
